name: Cat Anderson
abstract: William Alonzo "Cat" Anderson (September 12, 1916 – April 29, 1981) was an American jazz trumpeter known for his long period as a member of Duke Ellington's orchestra and for his wide range, especially his ability to play in the altissimo register.

works.t: Things Ain't What They Used To Be
Second Sacred Concert: The Shepherd (Who Watches Over the Night Flock)

concerts.label: Duke Ellington in His Concert of Sacred Music
Newport Jazz Festival: Tribute to Lionel Hampton

about_quotes.speaker: Jack Sheldon
about_quotes.evidence: He worked with me a lot after I had him in a small band with Art Pepper and Cat Anderson and Tricky Lofton and Dave Frishberg and Nick Ceroli and Joe Mondragon and Jack Marshall.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack Sheldon interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Quentin Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: Cat Anderson came back. ... Cat came back in the band. ... Willie Cook came in the band for a while and Clark Terry and Ray Nance and Cat Anderson.

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: if I was not playing the lead part on a certain tune, and Cat Anderson didn’t show up, I fit in that slot.
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Erskine Hawkins
about_quotes.evidence: For one while, I had 5 trumpets including Cat Anderson. Cat Anderson was with the Sunset Royal band.

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: I've used him a few times. I went up to Canada here in '73, and I took him up there with me

about_quotes.speaker: Jay McShann
about_quotes.evidence: Yes, we had Buddy Tate, Cat Anderson, Gene Ramey, Gus Johnson, the old rhythm section.
